entry.title: Washington Post: 23 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Russia-Ukraine war live updates: ‘Scorched earth’ tactics in eastern city; soldier’s war crimes verdict due
  • Biden says quarantines for monkeypox aren’t needed in U.S.
  • ‘Russia House’ in Davos becomes war crimes exhibit ahead of Zelensky address
  • In shadow of war, there’s no ‘business as usual’ in Davos
  • Global health talks clouded by conspiracy theories about pandemic treaty
  • A grasp at diplomacy as fighting grinds on in Ukraine
  • Ahead of Biden visit, Israel launches biggest eviction of Palestinians in decades
  • Ukrainian leaders warn of dire situation in key eastern city of Severodonetsk

  • Ukraine’s first lady details war’s toll on the Zelensky family
  • Biden visit tests new South Korean president, a foreign policy novice
  • Security concerns, lack of support stall Africa's Green Wall
  • 'They ruined everything': Fleeing the devastation in Ukraine

  • 7 die in Philippine ferry fire; over 120 rescued from water

  • Cannes: Transylvania-set 'R.M.N.' probes a ubiquitous crisis
  • Iran Revolutionary Guard colonel is shot dead in Tehran
  • Leclerc's perfect weekend in Spain ruined by faulty Ferrari
  • For members of Mother Emanuel, Buffalo shooting stirs painful memories
  • Two years after Floyd’s death, Black Minnesotans say little has changed
  • In new book, Kellyanne Conway takes aim at many targets — except Trump

  • From Sandy Hook to Buffalo: Ten years of failure on gun control
  • Live Nation subsidiaries got millions in aid meant for independent venues

  • The 1970s Had a Big Bright Side, Too

  • Bigger Shocks Are Coming With Your Electricity Bills
  • As Risks Rain Down on Emerging Markets, Unrest Rises
  • Why Wiping Out Smallpox Has Stoked Risk of Monkeypox

  • ‘Long covid’ is going to be a long haul
  • Why racism is bad for White people

  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
  • Chat with Jennifer Rubin about her columns, politics, policy and more

  • Contact the reader representative

Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 23 May, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 23 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-23 06:30AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Coronavirus: most young Hongkongers should ‘absolutely not’ get fourth Covid shot, vaccine expert says

  • China’s young scientists build academic journal from the ground up
  • China’s children of the 70s come to the fore as next generation of leaders starts to emerge
  • China’s appetite for Russian energy is being hit by diminishing consumption, soaring prices
  • German carmaker Audi, ad agency M&C Saatchi apologise for copyright infringement in video campaign with Hong Kong’s Andy Lau
  • Why Chinese demand for African imports might defy Covid lockdowns, supply chain snarls
  • China drums up support for global security push in Latin America as US looks to Asia
  • Chinese space scientists aim for telescope to find next Earth up to 32 light years away
  • Australia’s new PM Albanese has chance to mend fences with China, analysts say
  • China pledges US$1.5b in grain farmer subsidies to offset soaring production costs
  • Maskless North Korean leader Kim Jong-un carries coffin at state funeral amid Covid-19 outbreak
  • US and Japan committed to defending Taiwan if it’s taken by force, Joe Biden says
  • Philippine ferry fire leaves at least 7 dead, dozens are rescued
  • Australia’s new PM Albanese heads to Tokyo, says relationship with China to remain ‘a difficult one’
  • Pakistan’s defiant ex-PM Imran Khan calls for protests to force elections
  • Return of ‘trashy tourists’ to Bali spotlights mass tourism’s double-edged sword
  • New Zealand PM Ardern to visit US to boost exports and lure tourists
  • Thailand: Bangkok votes in new governor for first time in almost a decade
  • Apec trade meeting ends without joint statement, remains apart on Russia
  • In Japan, Biden to launch economic plan for region sceptical on benefits
  • Will India’s demand for Malaysia’s palm oil dip after Indonesia export ban reversal?
  • South Korea’s Yoon Suk-yeol to unveil own Indo-Pacific strategy following summit with US President Joe Biden
  • Renowned Indian dancer Arunima Kumar to perform Kuchipudi for Britain’s Queen Elizabeth’s Platinum Jubilee
  • India’s export ban makes wheat cheap – hurting farmers already reeling from extreme heat
  • Forcibly displaced worldwide tops 100 million for the first time
  • The volunteers from Belarus, Russia’s ally, fighting for Ukraine
  • Iran says Guards colonel shot dead in Tehran ‘assassination’
  • Zelensky’s fleece jacket is in high demand, but won’t be restocked until Ukraine’s ‘victory’
  • Russia’s Putin will be in a sanatorium and out of power by 2023, ex-MI6 chief predicts
  • Drug makers propose swift pandemic response to benefit poorer countries
  • Storms kill at least 8 in Canada, leave half a million without power
  • A hot, deadly summer is coming with frequent blackouts worldwide
  • Ukraine war, pandemic on agenda at WHO international meeting
  • How concerned should we be about monkeypox?
  • Queen Elizabeth’s Jubilee parade to include corgis, horses, and swans
  • Ukraine rules out ceasefire as fighting intensifies in Donbas
  • Taliban order leads to once defiant Afghan women TV presenters covering their faces
  • WHO says ‘urgent need’ for package of measures against monkey pox
  • Ukraine war: fate of 2,500 POWs from Mariupol steel plant stirs concern
  • Covid-19, Ukraine war and property downturn all add to China’s economic policy challenges
  • Alibaba, Kuaishou pace Hong Kong stock losses on tech earnings outlook while Beijing faces tighter Covid rules
  • Asia-Pacific commercial real estate deals set for another record year as companies seek cash buffer after Covid crisis
  • HKEX to set up two overseas offices in US, Europe to attract more global IPOs, investment, CEO Aguzin says
  • Earnings pitfall awaits Meituan, Alibaba stock buyers as China lockdown blunts spending
  • Biden’s blurry Indo-Pacific economic vision brings few new ideas to benefit Asia
  • Covid-19, climate change and conflict have left Asia-Pacific at a crossroads
  • Biden’s Indo-Pacific economic plan is yet another example of ‘with us or against us’ oversimplification
  • HSBC CEO rebukes comments on climate change by top banker, says they are ‘inconsistent’ with lender’s strategy
  • Empowering children to become creators in the metaverse and embrace the new virtual age
  • POST EDIT: New Omega Seamaster collection unleashes watch tech from record-breaking dive
  • Cannes 2022: Hunt movie review – Korean spy thriller directed by and starring Squid Game star Lee Jung-jae proves bombastic and muddled
  • Popular Chinese god in Singapore and Malaysia turns up in a surprising location
  • K-drama Rose Mansion: Lim Ji-yeon stars in apartment-set mystery-horror, which trades story for gratuitous sleaze and violence
  • Balenciaga fashion show, attended by stars like Kanye, Offset and Megan Thee Stallion, is a sign of things to come at the luxury label
  • How travel and tourism are changing, says Hong Kong hotel group Dorsett, which is adjusting its offerings in response
  • Sailing Indonesia’s Raja Ampat archipelago: a place that deserves to be called paradise (especially after a good beach clean-up)
  • Cannes 2022: Return to Seoul movie review – adopted from South Korea, raised in France, a woman looks for biological parents in Davy Chou’s nuanced tale about searching for one’s past
  • Asian-Canadian millennials talk about travel during Covid, living through the pandemic and future overseas travel plans
  • What is neem? The health benefits of what Ayurveda calls the world’s most versatile medicinal plant, from boosting immunity to fighting cancer
  • The return of K-pop’s ‘bad boy’ Psy: his BTS Suga collaboration Take That is a hit, but how much do you know about then Gangnam Style singer’s wealthy family and controversial early career?
  • 3 luxury superyachts – and even a submarine – to cruise on this summer, from Aston Martin and Triton Submarines’ Project Neptune, to Beneteau Flyer 8 Spacedeck, Highfield SP800 and Karnic SL702 MK2
  • Do we need fashion weeks any more? How the metaverse, digital avatars and live-streamed catwalk shows are democratising the luxury landscape
  • What to know about Avatar: The Way of Water, releasing in December 2022: Vin Diesel, Kate Winslet and Michelle Yeoh will join the cast of James Cameron’s hotly awaited sequel
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.title: The Diplomat: 23 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Political Dynasties Dominate Philippines Election — Again
  • Reformist Independent Scores Decisive Win in Bangkok Governor Election
  • The Path to Militancy and Terrorism
  • Australia Has a New Government. What Should Quad Leaders Expect?
  • Looking Back at the 1999 India-Pakistan Lahore Declaration
  • Philippines Establishes 3 New Coast Guard Outposts in South China Sea

  • China’s ‘Little Gaokao’: A Choice for the Choiceless

  • US-ASEAN Summit a Missed Opportunity to Tackle Shrinking Civic Space Across the Region

  • The Geopolitical Consequences of China’s Solomon Islands Pact
  • Japan to Co-develop Future Fighter F-X With UK

  • Sri Lanka’s Meltdown Puts China’s Strategic Influence in Jeopardy
  • History Education and the Politics of Identity in a Globalizing Vietnam
  • Sizing-up the US-ASEAN Summit and Vietnam’s Place in the World
  • Activists Seek Supreme Court Intervention to Head Off Marcos Presidency
  • Beware the Himalayan Heat: China, India, and Pakistan on the Brink
  • Taiwan’s President Condemns California Church Shooting
  • Aidan Craney: Youth in Fiji and Solomon Islands
  • China’s Censors Aim to Contain Dissent During Harsh COVID-19 Lockdowns
  • Which Yoon Should Biden Expect at the Upcoming South Korea-US Summit?
  • India’s Pandemic Mortality: Lies, Damned Lies, and Statistics
  • Understanding the Extent (and Limits) of Chinese Public Support for Russia
  • Cyber Radicalization by Bangladeshi Islamists
  • Do Australian Politicians Know the Difference Between Hinduism and Hindutva?
  • The Economic Outlook for the Philippines Under Ferdinand Marcos Jr.
  • Assessing the Outcomes of the US-ASEAN Special Summit
  • Malaysian FM Meets With Counterpart from Myanmar Opposition Movement
  • Marcos’ Victory Offers Washington the Chance for a Diplomatic Reset with the Philippines
  • Why Taiwan Can’t Copy Ukraine’s Civil Defense Blueprint
  • Okinawa Marks 50 Years of End to US Rule Amid Protests
  • Taiwan Fights for Its Diplomatic Survival in Latin America
  • Ukraine War: The Impact on Japan-Russia Relations
  • South Korea Can Do More in the Battle Against COVID-19
  • What’s Behind China’s Ban on NFTs?
  • North Korea’s COVID-19 Crisis
  • Does the Russia-Ukraine War Herald a New Era for Japan’s Security Policy?
  • The Clock’s Ticking on Pakistan’s Economic Bailout
  • Macho Election Tactics Miss the Point in Australia
  • Strengthening the France-India Partnership
The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: Economist: 21 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • Business

  • Johnson: Enter the dragon app
  • Indicators: Economic data, commodities and markets

Global news and current affairs from a European perspective. Best downloaded on Friday mornings (GMT)

entry.title: Wired Magazine: 22 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • Yahya Abdul-Mateen II Is Ready to Blow Your Mind
  • It’s Time to Reimagine the Future of Cyberpunk
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: The Third Pole: 20 May, 2022
entry.summary: The Third Pole: 20 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-20 10:56AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Northern India caught in vicious cycle of heatwaves and forest fires
  • Northern Pakistan’s neglected peatlands are disappearing

  • Climate change exacerbates food crisis in Nepal’s poorest region
  • Floating solar: A small but vital role for India’s sunrise sector
  • Europe strives to end dependency on Russian gas

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.title: Nature: 19 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The toll of menopause: how universities can help
  • Nature journals raise the bar on sex and gender reporting in research
  • How climate law can help to prevent the next pandemic
  • The metrics that predict the course of type 2 diabetes
  • Mysterious cosmic thief is stealing a dwarf galaxy’s gas
  • The ocean is losing its memory
  • ‘Barcodes’ reveal a cancer’s life story
  • Gentle-giant sharks are on a collision course with mighty ships
  • Flower power: ‘Sunflower’ system churns out useful energy
  • Helium levels in the atmosphere are ballooning
  • Parasite names, mouse rejuvenation and toxic sunscreen
  • Black hole at the centre of our Galaxy imaged for the first time
  • China expands control over genetic data used in scientific research
  • This Arctic town wants to make renewable energy work at the top of the world
  • Coronavirus ‘ghosts’ found lingering in the gut
  • Origin of life theory involving RNA–protein hybrid gets new support
  • The pandemic’s true health cost: how much of our lives has COVID stolen?
  • How three Ukrainian scientists are surviving Russia’s brutal war
  • Rise of drug-resistant tuberculosis is hidden in plain sight
  • AI can burn or brighten, and intangible economics: Books in brief
  • Want to prevent pandemics? Stop spillovers
  • One statistical analysis must not rule them all
  • Childcare crowdfunding campaigns aim to keep mums on the academic track
  • Immune to frustrations
  • Giant collision created galaxies devoid of dark matter
  • Young cerebrospinal fluid improves memory in old mice
  • A lab-on-a-chip that takes the chip out of the lab
  • Killing the messenger to evade bacterial defences
  • Tension hones body segmentation around the clock
  • A trail of dark-matter-free galaxies from a bullet-dwarf collision
  • Direct observation of the dead-cone effect in quantum chromodynamics
  • Polariton Bose–Einstein condensate from a bound state in the continuum
  • Observation of a molecular bond between ions and Rydberg atoms
  • Microcomb-driven silicon photonic systems
  • Microfluidic chain reaction of structurally programmed capillary flow events
  • Programmable heating and quenching for efficient thermochemical synthesis
  • Intermolecular [2π+2σ]-photocycloaddition enabled by triplet energy transfer
  • Synergistic HNO3–H2SO4–NH3 upper tropospheric particle formation
  • Expanding ocean food production under climate change
  • A natural mutator allele shapes mutation spectrum variation in mice
  • Genetic and chemotherapeutic influences on germline hypermutation
  • Young CSF restores oligodendrogenesis and memory in aged mice via Fgf17
  • Left–right symmetry of zebrafish embryos requires somite surface tension
  • Phage anti-CBASS and anti-Pycsar nucleases subvert bacterial immunity
  • Group A Streptococcus induces GSDMA-dependent pyroptosis in keratinocytes
  • Landscape of helper and regulatory antitumour CD4+ T cells in melanoma
  • Selective inhibition of miRNA processing by a herpesvirus-encoded miRNA
  • Nonlinear mechanics of human mitotic chromosomes
  • Design of protein-binding proteins from the target structure alone
  • Structural basis of NPR1 in activating plant immunity
  • USP14-regulated allostery of the human proteasome by time-resolved cryo-EM
  • Author Correction: The guidance receptor plexin D1 is a mechanosensor in endothelial cells
  • Publisher Correction: Signatures of TOP1 transcription-associated mutagenesis in cancer and germline
  • Addendum: Large metallicity variations in the Galactic interstellar medium
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: Scientific American: June 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • U.S. Kids Are Falling behind Global Competition, but Brain Science Shows How to Catch Up

  • How Mammals Conquered the World after the Asteroid Apocalypse

  • Tuberculosis Is the Oldest Pandemic, and Poverty Makes It Continue

  • Mammals' Perseverance, Fast Radio Bursts and Health Justice
  • Acne Inflammation Discovery Could Lead to New Treatments

  • Most of Us Combine Personality Traits from Different Genders

Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: Time: May 23rd, 2022 | Vol. 199, No. 19 | U.S.

entry.content:

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 23 May, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 23 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-23 00:58AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • New Book Announcement: “Beethoven in Beijing: Stories from the Philadelphia Orchestra’s Historic Journey to China” by Jennifer Lin
  • “Silent Parade” by Keigo Higashino
  • “Chariot of the Sun: An Informal History of a Siamese Family” by Shane Bunnag
  • “The Bangalore Detectives Club” by Harini Nagendra
  • Podcast with Glynne Walley, translator of “Eight Dogs, or ‘Hakkenden’: Part One—An Ill-Considered Jest” by Kyokutei Bakin
  • New Book Announcement: “Stone Masters: Power Encounters in Mainland Southeast Asia”, edited by Holly High
  • “Vernacular English: Reading the Anglophone in Postcolonial India” by Akshya Saxena
  • “Breathless” by Amy McCulloch
  • Eliot and I
  • “Everest 1922: The Epic Story of the First Attempt on the World’s Highest Mountain” by Mick Conefrey
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.

entry.content:

Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers
